name: Chocolate
dangerous_amount: 20mg theobromine per kg body weight causes toxicity; dark chocolate and baking chocolate are most dangerous (130-450mg/oz); milk chocolate has less (44-58mg/oz)
first_aid: Contact vet immediately. Do not induce vomiting unless directed by a vet. The vet may administer activated charcoal.

name: Grapes and Raisins
dangerous_amount: Any amount can be toxic. No safe threshold has been established. Even a single grape has caused kidney failure in small dogs.
first_aid: Contact vet or ASPCA Poison Control immediately. Do not wait for symptoms. Induced vomiting may be recommended if ingestion was recent.

name: Onions
dangerous_amount: All forms toxic: raw, cooked, powdered, dehydrated. Toxic at 0.5% of body weight for a single ingestion; cumulative toxicity also occurs.
first_aid: Contact vet. Induced vomiting if recent ingestion. Supportive care and potentially blood transfusions for severe anemia.

name: Garlic
dangerous_amount: More toxic than onions on a per-weight basis. Toxic dose approximately 15-30g per kg body weight. All forms (raw, cooked, powdered) are dangerous.
first_aid: Contact vet immediately. Inducing vomiting may help if ingestion was recent. Blood tests to monitor for anemia.

name: Xylitol
dangerous_amount: 0.1g per kg causes hypoglycemia; 0.5g per kg may cause liver failure. Found in sugar-free gum, candy, peanut butter, baked goods, and some medications.
first_aid: EMERGENCY — contact vet or animal poison control immediately. Do not induce vomiting. Requires urgent intravenous dextrose and liver monitoring.

name: Avocado
dangerous_amount: The leaves, bark, skin, and pit contain the highest persin concentration. The flesh is lower risk but still not safe in large quantities. Guatemalan variety is most toxic.
first_aid: Contact vet. Monitor for respiratory distress or cardiac symptoms. Pit ingestion also poses a choking and intestinal blockage hazard.

name: Macadamia Nuts
dangerous_amount: As few as 2.4g per kg body weight can cause symptoms. Symptoms typically appear within 12 hours of ingestion.
first_aid: Contact vet. Supportive care including IV fluids and temperature management. Most dogs recover within 24-48 hours with treatment.

name: Alcohol
dangerous_amount: Dogs are far more sensitive to ethanol than humans. Even small amounts of beer, wine, or spirits can cause serious harm. Sugar-free alcoholic drinks pose additional xylitol risk.
first_aid: Contact vet immediately. Treatment includes IV fluids, temperature regulation, and monitoring blood glucose and respiratory function.

name: Caffeine
dangerous_amount: Toxic dose approximately 140mg per kg. Sources include coffee, tea, energy drinks, diet pills, some medications, and cocoa. Even coffee grounds or tea bags are dangerous.
first_aid: Contact vet immediately. Induced vomiting if recent ingestion. Supportive care with IV fluids and medications to control heart rate and seizures.

name: Raw Yeast Dough
toxic_compound: Ethanol (produced by yeast fermentation) and carbon dioxide gas
dangerous_amount: Any amount is dangerous. Dough expands in the warm stomach, causing gas and ethanol production. Even small amounts can cause gastric dilatation volvulus (GDV).
first_aid: EMERGENCY — contact vet immediately. May require surgery. IV fluids and ethanol treatment. Do not try to make the dog vomit.

name: Cooked Bones
dangerous_amount: Any cooked bone is a hazard. Cooking makes bones brittle, causing them to splinter into sharp shards. Chicken, pork, and fish bones are especially dangerous.
first_aid: Monitor closely for choking, pain, blood in stool, or vomiting. Contact vet immediately if any symptoms appear. May require endoscopy or surgery to remove fragments.

name: Salt and Salty Snacks
dangerous_amount: Toxic at approximately 4g of salt per kg body weight. Chips, pretzels, popcorn, and salted crackers can accumulate to harmful levels, especially in small dogs.
first_aid: Provide plenty of fresh water and contact vet. IV fluids may be needed for sodium ion poisoning. Monitor sodium levels carefully.

name: Nutmeg
dangerous_amount: One to two teaspoons can cause toxicity. Commonly found in baked goods, eggnog, and spice blends. Small amounts may only cause mild upset.
first_aid: Contact vet if more than a small amount was ingested. Supportive care for heart rate and neurological symptoms.

name: Cherries
dangerous_amount: The flesh itself is not highly toxic, but the pit, stem, and leaves contain cyanide. A few pits can cause poisoning, particularly in small dogs.
first_aid: Contact vet if pits were consumed. Monitor for signs of respiratory distress or shock. Cyanide poisoning requires immediate veterinary treatment.

name: Green Tomatoes and Tomato Plants
dangerous_amount: Ripe tomato flesh is generally safe in small amounts, but unripe tomatoes and all parts of the tomato plant are toxic. Stems and leaves are most concentrated.
first_aid: Contact vet if the dog ate plant material or unripe tomatoes. Supportive care for GI symptoms.

name: Raw Potatoes
dangerous_amount: Green, unripe, or raw potatoes and potato plant leaves contain solanine. Cooked plain potatoes are generally safe in small amounts.
first_aid: Contact vet if large amounts of green or raw potato were eaten. Monitor for neurological and GI symptoms.

name: Mushrooms (Wild)
toxic_compound: Varies by species — amatoxins, muscarine, ibotenic acid, and others
dangerous_amount: Some wild mushroom species are deadly in very small amounts. Amanita phalloides (death cap) is extremely toxic. Avoid all wild mushrooms.
first_aid: EMERGENCY — contact vet or poison control immediately. If possible, take a photo or sample of the mushroom. Induced vomiting and activated charcoal may be used.

name: Rhubarb
dangerous_amount: The leaves contain the highest concentration and are most toxic. The stalk is lower risk but still not recommended. Can cause kidney failure in large amounts.
first_aid: Contact vet. Rinse mouth with water. IV fluids may be needed to protect kidney function.

name: Walnuts
dangerous_amount: Black walnuts and mouldy walnuts are especially dangerous. Regular English walnuts are less toxic but still not recommended due to high fat content and potential mold.
first_aid: Contact vet. Monitor for tremors or seizures. Treatment may include induced vomiting, activated charcoal, and anticonvulsant medications.

name: Pecans
dangerous_amount: Not recommended in any amount. High fat content can cause pancreatitis. Mouldy pecans can produce tremor-causing mycotoxins.
first_aid: Contact vet if significant amount consumed, especially if mouldy. Monitor for GI distress and neurological symptoms.

name: Almonds
toxic_compound: No specific toxin — mechanical and gastrointestinal hazard
dangerous_amount: Not acutely toxic like grapes, but the high fat and salt content in processed almonds causes GI upset. Whole almonds pose a choking hazard and can obstruct the intestine.
first_aid: Monitor for GI symptoms. Contact vet if the dog ate many almonds or shows signs of pancreatitis (vomiting, abdominal pain, lethargy).

name: Apple Seeds
dangerous_amount: A few seeds are unlikely to cause harm. Chronic consumption or large amounts can release enough cyanide to cause poisoning. Apple flesh is safe.
first_aid: Contact vet if large number of seeds were consumed. The apple flesh is safe to feed in moderation.

name: Peach Pits
toxic_compound: Amygdalin (cyanide precursor) and physical obstruction hazard
symptoms: Shock from cyanide in large amounts
dangerous_amount: The flesh is generally safe in small amounts. The pit is dangerous both as a physical obstruction and as a source of cyanide. Wilted peach leaves are also toxic.
first_aid: Contact vet if pit was swallowed. May require endoscopy or surgery. Monitor for cyanide symptoms if the pit was chewed.

name: Plum Pits
dangerous_amount: The pit, stem, and leaves contain cyanide compounds. Flesh is generally safe in small amounts. Pits also pose intestinal obstruction risk.
first_aid: Contact vet if pit was ingested. Monitor for cyanide poisoning symptoms. May require surgery for obstruction.

name: Lemons and Limes
toxic_compound: Psoralen compounds and essential oils (limonene, linalool)
dangerous_amount: The fruit flesh causes GI upset. The peel, seeds, and plant parts (leaves, stems) contain higher concentrations of toxic compounds.
first_aid: Most dogs will vomit on their own. Contact vet if skin reactions or neurological symptoms develop.

name: Grapefruits
dangerous_amount: Skin and seeds are more toxic than the flesh. Large amounts of peel are the main concern.
first_aid: Contact vet if more than a small amount was consumed. Generally self-limiting GI symptoms.

name: Currants
toxic_compound: Unknown — suspected similar mechanism to grapes
dangerous_amount: All types of currants (black, red, white) are suspected to carry similar toxicity to grapes. No safe threshold known.
first_aid: Treat as a grape/raisin emergency. Contact vet immediately. Induced vomiting if recent ingestion.

name: Chives
dangerous_amount: Member of the Allium family alongside onion and garlic. All forms (fresh, dried, cooked) are toxic. Toxic at similar thresholds to onion (0.5% body weight).
first_aid: Contact vet. Blood work to check for anemia. Supportive care.

name: Leeks
dangerous_amount: Part of the Allium family. Toxic in all forms. Less commonly consumed by dogs but dangerous at similar levels to onion.
first_aid: Contact vet. Blood tests to monitor red blood cell damage. Supportive care or transfusion if severe anemia.

name: Shallots
toxic_compound: Organosulfur compounds — more concentrated than onion
dangerous_amount: More potent than regular onions. All forms toxic. A smaller quantity can cause the same degree of toxicity as a larger amount of onion.
first_aid: Contact vet immediately. Same treatment protocol as onion toxicity.

name: Tobacco and Nicotine Products
dangerous_amount: Toxic at 0.5-1mg nicotine per kg. Cigarettes contain 9-30mg of nicotine each. E-cigarette liquid (vape juice) is especially dangerous as it contains concentrated nicotine.
first_aid: EMERGENCY — contact vet or poison control immediately. Induced vomiting if directed. Treatment for seizures and cardiac symptoms.

name: Marijuana / Cannabis
dangerous_amount: Dogs are more sensitive to THC than humans. Edibles containing chocolate or xylitol pose a compounded risk. CBD alone is lower risk but still not proven safe.
first_aid: Contact vet. Be honest about exposure — vets are not required to report this. Supportive care is the main treatment.

name: Hops
dangerous_amount: Used in home beer brewing. Even small amounts of spent or dried hops can be deadly. Certain breeds (Greyhounds, Labrador Retrievers) may be especially sensitive.
first_aid: EMERGENCY — contact vet immediately. Requires aggressive cooling and supportive care. Rapid fatality possible.

name: Yeast Extract (Marmite / Vegemite)
symptoms: Sodium ion toxicity in large amounts
dangerous_amount: Not acutely toxic in small amounts but the extremely high salt content makes it unsafe in any meaningful quantity, especially for small dogs.
first_aid: Provide fresh water. Contact vet if large amounts consumed. Monitor for signs of sodium toxicity.

name: Nutmeg (large quantities)
dangerous_amount: Trace amounts in baked goods unlikely to cause harm. 1-2 teaspoons of nutmeg is toxic for most dogs. Ground nutmeg in a shared dessert warrants monitoring.
first_aid: Contact vet if more than a small pinch was ingested. Supportive care for cardiac and neurological symptoms.

name: Corn Cobs
toxic_compound: Physical obstruction hazard — no toxic compound
dangerous_amount: Corn kernels are safe for dogs. The cob is the danger — its fibrous, dense texture resists digestion and can cause a life-threatening intestinal obstruction.
first_aid: Contact vet immediately if a cob or large piece was swallowed. Often requires endoscopy or surgery to remove.

name: Fruit Stones and Pits (General)
symptoms: Signs of cyanide poisoning if chewed
dangerous_amount: Applies to apricot, cherry, mango, and plum pits. Physical blockage risk from any large pit. Cyanide risk if the pit is crushed or chewed.
first_aid: Contact vet if a pit was swallowed. X-ray may be needed to assess blockage. Monitor for cyanide symptoms.

name: Milk and Dairy (in excess)
dangerous_amount: Most adult dogs are lactose intolerant to varying degrees. Small amounts of plain yogurt or hard cheese may be tolerated, but milk and ice cream commonly cause GI upset.
first_aid: Not a poison emergency. Provide water and bland diet. Contact vet if diarrhea is severe or prolonged.

name: Ice Cream
toxic_compound: Lactose and sugar; xylitol in sugar-free varieties
dangerous_amount: Regular ice cream is a GI irritant due to dairy and sugar. Sugar-free varieties may contain xylitol, which is severely toxic.
first_aid: Check the label for xylitol. If present, treat as xylitol emergency. Otherwise, monitor for GI symptoms and provide water.

name: Chewing Gum (Sugar-Free)
dangerous_amount: Just a few sticks of xylitol-containing gum can be fatal for small dogs. Brands including Trident, Extra, and Orbit commonly contain xylitol.
first_aid: EMERGENCY — contact vet immediately. Do not wait for symptoms. Requires IV dextrose and liver monitoring.

name: Peanut Butter (with Xylitol)
dangerous_amount: Several popular and store-brand peanut butter products use xylitol as a sweetener. Always read the label. Regular unsalted peanut butter is generally safe.
first_aid: EMERGENCY if xylitol is listed. Contact vet immediately. Check the label before giving any peanut butter product.

name: Mouldy Food
toxic_compound: Mycotoxins (tremorgenic mycotoxins from Penicillium and Aspergillus molds)
dangerous_amount: Any mouldy food is dangerous. Blue cheese, mouldy bread, spoiled nuts, and compost scraps are common culprits. Effects can be rapid and severe.
first_aid: Contact vet or poison control immediately. Induced vomiting may be appropriate if very recent. Anticonvulsants for seizures. IV fluids for support.

name: Raw Meat with Listeria / Salmonella
dangerous_amount: Risk varies by source and handling. Puppies, elderly dogs, immunocompromised dogs, and households with young children or pregnant women are at highest risk.
first_aid: Contact vet if dog shows signs of illness. Note that infected dogs can shed Salmonella to humans without appearing ill. Hand washing is critical.

name: Coffee Grounds
dangerous_amount: Coffee grounds contain highly concentrated caffeine. Even a small amount of grounds can be dangerous to a small dog. Instant coffee is also highly concentrated.
first_aid: Contact vet immediately. Induced vomiting if very recent. IV fluids and cardiac monitoring.

name: Tea (strong or large amounts)
dangerous_amount: A small accidental lick of cooled tea is unlikely to cause harm, but larger amounts and especially tea bags (concentrated caffeine) are concerning.
first_aid: Contact vet if a significant amount of tea or a tea bag was consumed. Monitor for caffeine toxicity symptoms.

name: Energy Drinks
dangerous_amount: Even a small amount is very dangerous due to high caffeine concentration. Some energy drinks also contain xylitol.
first_aid: Contact vet immediately. Check label for xylitol. Treat as caffeine emergency.

name: Xylitol in Medications
dangerous_amount: Found in some chewable vitamins, liquid medications, nasal sprays, and throat lozenges. Always check inactive ingredients before giving any human product to a dog.
first_aid: EMERGENCY — contact vet or ASPCA Poison Control. Requires immediate blood glucose monitoring and IV dextrose.

name: Baking Powder
dangerous_amount: Toxic at approximately 10-20g per kg. A small dog eating raw cookie dough or unbaked goods could ingest a dangerous amount.
first_aid: Contact vet. Monitor electrolyte levels. IV fluids for serious cases.

name: Baking Soda (large amounts)
dangerous_amount: Toxic dose approximately 10-20g per kg. Not dangerous in trace amounts in cooked food, but concerning if a dog eats raw dough or spilled quantities.
first_aid: Contact vet if more than a trivial amount was consumed. Monitor for electrolyte imbalance.

name: Blue-Green Algae (water)
dangerous_amount: Any contact or ingestion is an emergency. Found in warm, stagnant ponds and lakes in summer. Even licking fur after swimming in affected water can be fatal.
first_aid: EMERGENCY — contact vet immediately. Rinse dog with clean water if it swam in suspected bloom. There is no antidote; treatment is supportive.

name: Human Vitamins with Iron
dangerous_amount: Iron toxicity occurs at approximately 20mg per kg. Prenatal vitamins contain particularly high iron levels. Keep all human vitamins away from dogs.
first_aid: Contact vet or poison control. Induced vomiting if recent. May require chelation therapy for serious cases.

name: Ibuprofen and NSAIDs
dangerous_amount: Ibuprofen is toxic at as little as 25mg per kg. A single standard 200mg tablet can cause toxicity in a small dog. Never give human pain medication to dogs.
first_aid: EMERGENCY — contact vet or poison control immediately. Induced vomiting, activated charcoal, IV fluids, and kidney monitoring required.
